title: Tulsi Gabbard under scrutiny for showing up at FBI raid of Georgia election hub
summary: Tulsi Gabbard is drawing criticism for her decision to accompany FBI agents on a search at an election center in Fulton County, Georgia.
story: WASHINGTON — When President Donald Trump watched a live feed of the U.S. military operation that captured Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro, the director of national intelligence, Tulsi Gabbard, was not in the room. Just two days earlier, she had posted photos of herself on a beach in Hawaii at sunset practicing yoga, sending out a new year’s greeting for “peace.”
That she appeared to be on vacation in the run-up to such a high-stakes, ultra-sensitive military operation seemed to underscore the extent to which she has been sidelined by the administration.

But there she was on Wednesday, at an election center in Fulton County, Georgia, as FBI agents conducted a raid seeking ballots from the 2020 presidential election, which Trump has falsely claimed he won.
Gabbard's presence baffled national security experts and raised questions about whether she is seeking new relevancy in the eyes of a president who had soured on her.

“Even if there was some criminal activity in the 2020 election in Georgia — despite repeated investigations that show there was none — it’s still a purely domestic problem — not one involving foreign nations,” a former national security official said. “The director of national intelligence has nothing to do with this."

Accompanying FBI agents on a raid is unprecedented for the chief of U.S. intelligence, whose job is to track threats from foreign adversaries. In her role overseeing the country’s spy agencies, Gabbard is prohibited by law from taking part in domestic law enforcement. Her predecessors took pains to keep their distance from Justice Department cases or partisan politics.

Asked about the rationale for her visit to Georgia, a senior administration official said: “Director Gabbard has a pivotal role in election security and protecting the integrity of our elections against interference, including operations targeting voting systems, databases, and election infrastructure.”
The official added: “She has and will continue to take action on President Trump’s directive to secure our elections and work with our interagency partners to do so.”

Two senior officials with knowledge of the matter said Gabbard’s presence in Fulton County was unnecessary and was not requested by the Justice Department. But they added that Gabbard was merely observing the execution of the FBI search warrant, and there was nothing illegal in her presence.
“It seems to be an attempt to make herself relevant," one official said. "It’s so strange."
Late Thursday, Trump responded to a reporter's question about Gabbard's presence at the raid.

"She’s working very hard on trying to keep the elections safe, and she’s done a very good job," Trump said at the Kennedy Center ahead of the premiere of a documentary about first lady Melania Trump. "You got a signed judge’s order in Georgia, and you’re going to see some interesting things happening. They’ve been trying to get there for a long time."

At a Senate Intelligence Committee hearing on Thursday, Sen. Mark Warner, D-Va., vice chair of the Senate Intelligence Committee, sharply criticized Gabbard’s decision to travel to Fulton County for the FBI search, saying it was part of a pattern of senior officials blurring the line between intelligence work and domestic politics.

Warner said there were only two explanations for national intelligence director’s trip: either Gabbard believed the case had a link to foreign intelligence, and she failed to abide by her legal obligation to inform congressional committees about it, or she was tarnishing the nonpartisan reputation of the intelligence agencies with a “domestic political stunt designed to legitimize conspiracy theories that undermine our democracy.”
"Either scenario," Warner added, "represents a serious breach of trust and a dereliction of duty to the solemn office which she holds."

In a letter to Gabbard, Warner and the senior Democrat on the House Intelligence Committee, Rep. Jim Himes of Connecticut, demanded the national intelligence director brief lawmakers as part of her legal obligation to keep the committees fully informed about relevant intelligence matters.

“It is also deeply concerning that you participated in this domestic law enforcement action,” the two lawmakers said in a letter to Gabbard. “The Intelligence Community should be focused on foreign threats and, as you yourself have testified, when those intelligence authorities are turned inwards the results can be devastating for Americans privacy and civil liberties.”

During Trump’s first term in office, his administration set up an effort across the government to identify and counter foreign adversaries trying to undermine U.S. elections. But Gabbard last year dismantled a center designed to track foreign actors seeking to interfere in American elections or institutions, and the Justice Department and State Department also shut down similar offices.

Over the past year, Gabbard has clashed with two of her counterparts in the administration, FBI Director Kash Patel and CIA Director John Ratcliffe, in what officials described as a struggle for influence inside the administration.

In October, the FBI strongly objected to a proposal by some House lawmakers to strip the bureau of its authority over counterintelligence operations and hand them over to Gabbard’s office, warning that the plan would sow confusion and undercut national security. In the end, the FBI retained its primary authority over counterintelligence.
The search of the Fulton County election center follows Trump’s repeated baseless claims that the 2020 election result was fraudulent and that he was the legitimate winner, not Joe Biden.

During his remarks this month at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Trump raised the issue with foreign leaders and corporate chiefs in the audience, saying, “People will soon be prosecuted for what they did.”

Trump and his supporters have offered various theories — without detailed evidence — as to why they claim the 2020 election was “rigged,” none of which held up in a series of court cases. Some Trump allies have falsely claimed foreign actors were involved, without providing details.

“It is unclear what the foreign intelligence nexus is to the service of an FBI search warrant on a board of elections in Atlanta,” said Kevin Carroll, a former CIA officer and now a lawyer handling national security cases. “Absent such a nexus, ODNI’s involvement in the matter is wrong and potentially even illegal" if she took part in the search.

He added: “It is also inappropriate for a Cabinet-level official to take part in a law enforcement operation. Among other things, the director is now potentially a fact witness in any suppression hearing or trial related to the evidence seized by the bureau.”

title: Genetics play a larger part in lifespan than previously thought
summary: A person’s genes play a far greater role in likely lifespan than previously thought, according to a major new study published Thursday in the journal Science
story: A person’s genes play a far greater role in likely lifespan than previously thought, according to a major new study published Thursday in the journal Science.
Using data from human twin studies, an international team of researchers arrived at the conclusion that the genetic contribution to how long we’re likely to live is as high as 55%.

This new finding is strikingly higher than previous estimates, which have calculated the role of genetics in lifespan could range from 6% to 33%. It’s likely to intrigue — and perhaps disappoint — the fast-growing community of longevity influencers and self-described biohackers touting longer lives through supplements and customized drug regimens.

The study authors said they arrived at this very different figure by separating out what they termed extrinsic mortality (defined as deaths from external factors such as accidents, homicides, environmental hazards and infectious diseases) and intrinsic mortality (deaths caused by internal biological factors such as age-related diseases, genetic mutations and the general decline of health with age).

Through treating these two categories of death separately, the researchers said they were able to get a far more accurate estimate of the relationship between genetics and lifespan. It also matches with findings regarding the role of genes in other key physiological traits: Height, body fat distribution and muscle build are all thought to be at least 50% heritable.

“The number that we got is not out of nowhere,” said lead study author Ben Shenhar, who researches the physics of aging at the Weizmann Institute of Science in Rehovot, Israel. “If you look at twin studies on pretty much anything in humans, you get this 50%. If you look at the heritability of age of onset at menopause, which is an age-related decline, that is also around 50%.”

Morten Scheibye-Knudsen, an associate professor in the department of cellular and molecular medicine at the University of Copenhagen, described the new approach as a means of “eliminating the outside noise” to unmask the underlying biology of aging. Scheibye-Knudsen, who wrote an editorial published alongside the study, said that through looking at other species, it’s already clear that genes play a significant role in determining lifespan.

“We live [a maximum of] 120 years, and a yeast cell lives 13 days, and bowhead whales live 200 years,” he said. “So we already know our genes have set a limit to our lifespan, as it is now. I think people should have thought a little bit more about that because it cannot only be our behavior.”

Eric Verdin, president and chief executive of the Buck Institute for Research on Aging in California, who was not involved in the study, cautioned that the novel approach the researchers used may not be so clear-cut. Susceptibility to illness and death from infections such as Covid or flu could arguably be considered as intrinsic mortality as such vulnerability is at least partially genetic.

“We know that your genes play an enormous role in how you respond to infection,” said Verdin, who suggested that tweaking the analysis might slightly lower the apparent genetic contribution to lifespan.

Shenhar said that the researchers had considered this and re-ran their analysis, this time accounting for the fact that vulnerability to infections and falls rises as we get older. The findings still revealed that genetic makeup contributes to around 50% of life expectancy.

Shenhar said that the findings underscored the importance of continuing to search for key genes that confer longevity, pointing to other studies that have shown that centenarians appear to carry a lower risk of chronic diseases compared with the average person.
“It’s clear that these people are not just clawing their way to 100,” he said. “No, they have protective genes that protect against the harms of age.”

Verdin said the new study confirmed previous research showing that protective genes appeared to play a particularly crucial role in protecting some people against cardiovascular disease. However, so far only a handful of genetic variants associated with longevity have been discovered, notably versions of the FOXO3, APOE and SIRT6 genes.

“The search has not been very fruitful,” he said. “There are multiple genes that have been shown to be associated with aging, but if you study many centenarians, you don’t find these genes in all of them.”

Verdin continued: “To me this indicates that it comes down to multiple genes interacting with each other, and the key question is identifying which pairs or triplets [of gene variants] which yield longevity. One important step in trying to do this is to sequence the genomes of as many centenarians as possible.”
Shenhar agreed that the secret to a long life is likely to be dictated by multiple genes working in tandem, not one single gene “to rule them all.”
Still, while genetics may play a larger role in lifespan than previously thought, a healthy lifestyle can’t be discounted.

“The depressing thing about this is that it makes people be fatalistic,” Verdin said. “‘It doesn’t matter what I do. Why should I try to live better and not drink and do sport if it’s determined by genes basically?’”
Shenhar said he hopes this isn’t the main takeaway. If genetics dictate 55% of our life expectancy, that means lifestyle still makes up the remaining 45%, a not insignificant proportion.

“The message of our paper is not that lifestyle, exercise and diet are not important,” he said. “That is not our message, not at all. Even if your genetics gives you a particular potential or range for what your natural lifespan would be, depending on lifestyle, that might shift slightly one way or another. So it’s still important.”

title: ‘The Traitors’ Season 4, Episode 7 Recap: Who Was Banished and Who Was Murdered?
summary: After last week’s teaser featuring host Alan Cumming inviting the players to his murderous banquet, fans were eagerly anticipating how the episode would unfold. Find out what happened in Episode 7.
story: After last week’s teaser featuring host Alan Cumming inviting the players to his murderous banquet, fans were eagerly anticipating how the episode would unfold. But did it live up to the hype?

The seventh episode of Season 4, titled “The Black Banquet,” arrived on Peacock Jan. 29 and immediately welcomed the players and the viewers to the dinner party Cumming mentioned in the previous episode. To recap, Cumming tasked the Traitors — “The Real Housewives of Beverly Hills” alum Lisa Rinna former “The Real Housewives of Potomac” star Candiace Dillard Bassett and “Love Island USA” Season 6’s Rob Rausch — with committing their next murder in plain sight. The host gifted them an amulet that one of them had to wear. If one of the players touched the cursed accessory, they would be murdered that night.
Rinna stepped up and decided she would be the one to complete the task. So was she successful? Find out below in a recap of the latest episode, which also breaks down a betrayal at the Round Table.
The Banquet Begins

All the players arrive dressed to the nines for Cumming’s banquet. But as he mentioned, the Traitors are “dressed to kill.” “Survivor” winner Yamil “Yam Yam” Arocho is already on high alert and says he will avoid taking drinks or food from anyone, expecting a twist is on the way.

Meanwhile, Rinna is looking forward to pulling off the challenging task. “This is like the Super Bowl of murders,” she says in a voiceover, adding that she wants the event to be “the most iconic banquet in ‘Traitor’’s history.” She reveals that her shortlist for murder includes Arocho, fellow “Survivor” winner Natalie Anderson and “Top Chef” host Kristen Kish.

As the players fix their plates and notice different items around the room, like a set of tarot cards with their faces, many suspect that the Traitors are up to something. Viewers then learn in a confessional from Rinna that the deed is done and she has already chosen her victim.

After the group goes around the table and says how much they have enjoyed getting to know one other, there is a forceful knock at the door and Cumming storms in with two masked, hooded figures. He takes a seat at the table and tells the players “the legend of the black dinner.” He explains that in 1440 Scottish King James II invited a noble clan to a castle. Unbeknownst to them, it was a trap. “Well, I’m afraid my dear players that tonight is a little case of history repeating,” he says.

Cumming shares that the cursed player could be saved, before his guards present the players with two vials filled with an antidote. The host explains that if the cursed player drinks the antidote, then he or she will be saved. He gives them a short amount of time to decide which two players think they are the most vulnerable. Ultimately, a vocal Anderson drinks the antidote and Olympic figure skater Johnny Weir hands Kish the other vial.

But, the group chose incorrectly as Arocho is revealed to be the cursed player. As he is dragged away from the table by the guards, he uses his final words to call out Rinna, claiming a kiss from her marked him for death. She attempts to defend herself by claiming she welcomed multiple players with air kisses.
The Power of the Dagger Is Introduced

All three Traitors arrive first to breakfast the next morning. Rinna calls out Rausch for voting for her last episode but he pushes back, saying he didn’t want to blow his cover. Rinna asks both Rausch and Dillard Bassett to defend her following Arocho’s accusation.

After breakfast, Dillard Bassett joins a few other players in suspecting Anderson is a Traitor following her behavior at the banquet. But, Rausch continues to turn on his fellow Traitors and tells a group of players that Rinna looks guilty. He says that Arocho had no reason to lie during his final minutes.

At the mission, the players have to race up a steep hill, collecting gold along the way, to reach a monument. The players who collect the most gold will then compete for the dagger, which gives one player the power of two votes at a Round Table. The power can only be used once in the next four Round Tables.

Rausch says in his confessional that he wants the dagger so he can gain more power. As he makes his way up the hill, he reiterates to “The Bachelor” star Colton Underwood that Rinna cannot be trusted.

The players finish the mission and add $17,800 to the prize pot. The top six players who collected the most gold are Underwood, Rausch, “One Tree Hill” alum Stephen Colletti, Anderson, “Dancing With the Stars” pro Mark Ballas and singer Eric Nam.

Rausch collected the most gold so he has an advantage in the next round. The other five remaining players have to flip over a card which will reveal if they have the dagger. Rausch then will question all of them to determine who is lying. If he guesses correctly, he takes the card with the dagger. Rausch decides Anderson is being dishonest and he is correct. However, only Rausch learns that he won the dagger.
When they return to the castle, Rausch shares with the other five that he has the dagger. He tells them to keep it a secret between the six of them.
An OG Traitor Is Banished

During the Round Table discussion, Rinna and Weir bring up points about Anderson. But Underwood comes after Rinna, again, and Rausch backs him. Rausch says the group shouldn’t focus on their emotions and that logically it doesn’t make sense for Anderson to put a target on her back during the banquet.

The group votes and a majority write Rinna’s name, including Rausch. However, Dillard Bassett decides to throw some suspicion his way and votes for the “Love Island USA” star, explaining that she couldn’t vote for Rinna or Anderson.

After all the votes are revealed, a tearful Rinna stands on the Circle of Truth and says, “First of all, I have absolutely loved being here with all of you. I never thought I could do this. I never thought I could last this long. What I’m going to tell you tonight is, I am the most iconic, legendary, Traitor!”

In her final confessional, Rinna says through tears, “Rob, damn you. Damn you.” She adds, “You know, the end of ‘Housewives’ was very difficult for me. It was just so nice to be able to be me. I’m just so grateful for the entire experience. I really am. Thank you.”
Traitors’ Trust Is Broken
Back in the castle, the fractured trust between the Traitors is irreparable. Rausch is mad at Dillard Bassett for writing his name and the feeling is mutual.

“Ladies and gentlemen, we have a snake on our hands,” Dillard Bassett says in her confessional. “This was buffoonery of the most epic proportions. He is willing to betray the people that helped him. He made certain, vicious choices that will have dire consequences for him.”

As the episode ends, Underwood shifts his focus to Colletti but Weir is convinced “The Bachelor” star is the real Traitor. Weir is also suspicious of Rausch, who tells a group that it is possible the turret was full of “Housewives” stars, pointing fingers at Dillard Bassett and “The Real Housewives of New York City” alum Dorinda Medley.

Before meeting in the turret, Dillard Bassett tells the cameras, “Rob made a decision to disband the Traitors. The Christian woman in me is just clutching her pearls. I’m aghast. But the bad b---- that is Candiace is ready to play a m----------- game. I have to take the snake down and cut him off at the head.”
The episode concludes with Dillard Bassett and Rausch meeting in the turret. The fallout between the Traitors will continue with Episode 8, out Feb. 5.

title: Chappell Roan Says Her Grammy-Nominated Song 'The Subway' Was Inspired by Someone She Was 'In Love With'
summary: What do the lyrics of Chappell Roan's The Subway mean? HEre's what Chappell Roan said about the real relationship that inspired the song.
story: The lyrics to Chappell Roan's hit song "The Subway" will take you on a ride.
Released in July 2025, “The Subway” is nominated for two 2026 Grammys in the categories of best pop solo performance and record of the year.

Roan first teased “The Subway” at a live concert at the 2024 Governors Ball music festival while dressed as a New York taxi with her skin painted green to represent the Statue of Liberty. She released the official recorded version of the song over a year later.

Roan posted a lengthy caption on Instagram about the journey to the song’s release. She said in the year after its first performance, she “ripped (her) hair out trying to figure out the puzzle of how this song should feel musically and visually and emotionally.”
"Thank you for sticking it out for a whole year. It was worth it to make sure everything was absolutely right," she wrote.
The Meaning Behind the Song Lyrics of 'The Subway'

A melancholy tune, "The Subway" is about a girl reminiscing about a past relationship. Everywhere she goes, she's reminded of her lover — whether in green hair on the subway or someone wearing her perfume at a party.
The feelings linger and she's hoping, eventually, they'll fade: "But I’m still counting down all of the days/‘Til you’re just another girl on the subway
Roan said the song was inspired by "someone she was deeply in love with."

"It was actually about me hiding in Los Angeles from someone I was deeply in love with, but we weren't on bad terms. Like it was just trying to avoid the coffee shops we went to and parties," she told Apple Music in August 2025.

One of the lyrics went particularly viral: A mention of Saskatchewan. The verse explains that if Roan can't get over her relationship, she'll have to move out of New York — and chooses the province in Western Canada as her destination.
Roan said she simply chose it because it rhymed.
"We needed something 'on,'" Roan told Apple Music. "I've never been there, but I promise I will perform there one day. I also just love that the capital is called Regina."
Around halfway through the tune, the verse "she's got a way" is sung repeatedly — with minor, but important, variations. The lyrics go from "she's got a way" to "she got away."
In the music video, Roan chases a girl with green hair around New York. She keeps getting tangled in her own long hair, perhaps symbolizing being caught in her feelings.
Read the Lyrics to 'The Subway' in Full:
I saw your green hair, beauty mark next to your mouth
There, on the subway, I nearly had a breakdown
A few weeks later, somebody wore your perfume
It almost killed me, I had to leave the room
It's just another day and it's not over (Ah)
'Til it's over, it's never over
It's just another day and it's not over (Ah)
'Til it's over, it's never over
'Til I don't look for you on the staircase
Or wish you thought that we were still soulmates
But I'm still counting down all of the days
'Til you're just another girl on the subway
Made you the villain, evil for just moving on
I see your shadow, I see it even with the lights off
I made a promise if in four months, this feeling ain't gone
Well, f--k this city, I'm moving to Saskatchewan
It's just another day and it's not over (Ah)
'Til it's over, oh, 'til it's over
It's just another day and it's not over (Ah)
'Til it's over, it's never over
'Til I can break routine during foreplay
And trust myself that I won't say your name
But I'm still counting down all of the days
'Til you're just another girl on the subway
She's got, she's got a way
She's got a way, she's got a way
And she got, she got away
She got away, she got away
And she's got, she's got a way
She's got a way, she's got a way
And she got, she got away
She got away, she got away
(She's got, she's got a way
She's got a way, she's got a way)
She's got a way (She got, she got away
She got away, she got away)
She's got, she's got a way
She's got a way, she's got a way
And she got, she got away
She got away, she got away

title: Odessa A'Zion Responds to Fan Backlash, Drops Out of a Movie: 'I'm Out'
summary: "Marty Supreme" star Odessa A’zion dropped out of a movie Deep Cuts after backlash over her casting.
story: "Marty Supreme" star Odessa A’zion thanked fans for bringing a controversy to her attention and announced she is "out" on accepting a role that had sparked an online backlash.

A’zion, 25, was touted to be among the cast for the upcoming movie "Deep Cuts" in multiple reports only two days earlier. She shared in her Instagram Stories on Jan. 28 that she is no longer playing the role.
Here's what to know about the situation.
What Was the Controversy About Odessa A'zion's Role?
The upcoming A24 film "Deep Cuts" from director Sean Durkins is based on a 2025 novel by Holly Brickley of the same name.

A'zion was slated to play a character named Zoe Gutierrez, who is half Jewish and half Mexican in the novel. A'zion does not have Mexican heritage, which led some fans online to say the role was being whitewashed by giving it to a non-Hispanic actor.
A'zion noted in her Instagram Stories that she had never read the book, so she wasn't aware of the character's heritage.
The film also includes "Wake Up Dead Man" star Cailee Spaeny as music critic Percy Marks, and "Outer Banks" star Drew Starkey as songwriter Joe Morrow, the romantic interest of Spaeny's character.
What Did Odessa A'zion Say About Leaving the Film?

A'zion, who is the daughter of "Better Things" star Pamela Adlon and German director Felix Adlon, posted a series of messages on her Instagram stories after announcing she was withdrawing from the film.
"Guys!! I am with ALL of you and I am NOT doing this movie,” she initially posted. “F--- that. I’m OUT.”
A'zion then explained her decision.

"THANK YOU guys for bringing this to my attention. I AGREE WITH EVERY SINGLE ONE OF YOU!" she wrote. "This is why I love you guys. I’m so sorry that this happened. It is SO important for me to let you in on how it all went down: I went in for Percy, but was offered Zoe instead and instantly said yes! I’m so pissed y’all, I hadn’t read the book and should have paid more attention to all aspects of Zoe before accepting…and now that I know what I know???"
The "Fam" and "I Love LA" star then concluded by saying she looks forward to seeing who gets the role.

“I’d never take a role from someone else that’s meant to do it," she wrote. "That SHOULD do it! That’s not me. There are a plentitude of people more than capable of playing this role and I am NOT one of them. I can’t wait to see who it ends up being.”

title: ‘Bridgerton’ Showrunner on How Season 4 Will Embrace Benedict’s Sexuality
summary: Showrunner Jess Brownell discusses how Benedict Bridgerton's sexuality will continue to be embraced in Season 4.
story: Warning: This story contains spoilers for "Bridgerton" Season 4, Part 1.
Benedict Bridgerton's love life hasn't been the primary focus on the romantic Regency era show until now, but viewers have been paying close attention.

Since his introduction in Season 1, Luke Thompson's character has been a fan favorite, and his recurring pursuit of life outside of the traditional Ton society had many speculating Benedict may be queer. But it wasn't until Season 3, when he embarked on a relationship with Tilley Arnold and her friend Paul, that his sexuality was confirmed.

Thompson previously told Bustle that the best way to describe his character's sexuality in modern terms is pansexual, or "being attracted to the way that someone thinks and feels, regardless of gender," he explained.
"That’s a word that could be used. But what’s refreshing about it, certainly in the way that it’s being discovered at the moment, is that there is a sense of label-lessness about it," he said.

While he shares a brief kiss with a man in Episode 1 of Season 4, much of the season follows his love story with a woman named Sophie. But in Part 2, which is set to hit Netflix on Feb. 26,  showrunner Jess Brownell confirms his identity will continue to be explored.

"It's important for me that for any person who is queer — whether they're pansexual, bisexual, fluid, however they describe themselves — that even if that person ends up in a heterosexual presenting relationship, they are still queer," Brownell tells TODAY.com.

"It was really important to me and to the writers room to find ways to make it clear that Benedict still owns that part of himself," she adds. "So that is something that will be touched on here and in other seasons."
When discussing his character's pansexuality, Thompson said at the time that Benedict specifically approaches romance "in a spirit of curiosity."
That curiosity is also what's driving his pursuit of Sophie, or the “lady in silver,” after he meets the mystery woman at a masquerade ball in Episode 1.

“That life of dabbling is growing thin,” Brownell says of Benedict in Season 4. “And the moment he meets Sophie and sees the childlike wonder on her face as she takes in this ball, I think it is just this instant feeling of, ‘That is the feeling I’m missing in my life.’ Benedict has done it all and seen it all, and he’s growing jaded.
“So to meet someone who everything in society is new and exciting, is really exciting for him,” she adds.

title: Who Is Performing at the 2026 Grammys?
summary: Who is performing at the 2026 Grammys? Here's a list of everyone performing from Sabrina Carpenter to Pharrell Williams to best new artists like Olivia Dean.
story: Who is performing at the 2026 Grammys? The entire best new artist category, that's who.
As the Feb. 1 award ceremony approaches, the Recording Academy has been releasing the names of the performers viewers can expect to see take the stage at Crypto.com Arena in Los Angeles.

So far, the Recording Academy confirmed that all eight best new artist nominees will perform as part of a special segment. This will be the second year in a row that every artist in the category will sing.

So, who are all the best new artist nominees this year, and what other major artists have also been revealed as performers? Read on to learn everything you need to know about the 2026 Grammys performances, below.
Lady Gaga

Lady Gaga is no stranger to the Grammy stage. She will make a comeback this year for her 9th Grammy performance. The singer is heading into the award show with seven nominations from the Recording Academy. Gaga's latest album, "Mayhem," earned recognition for best pop vocal album and album of the year.

The album's hit song "Abracadabra" is nominated for record of the year, song of the year, and best dance pop of the year. It doesn't stop there — Gaga is nominated for best pop solo performance for her song "Disease" and best traditional pop vocal album for the "Joker" movie album "Harlequin."
Justin Bieber

Justin Bieber is making his grand return to the Grammy Awards after four years away. The Recording Academy revealed Jan. 28 that the pop star, who is nominated for four Grammys at this year's ceremony, is set to perform. Bieber was recognized for his latest album, Swag, which was released in July 2025. He is up for album of the year, best pop solo performance for his song "Daisies," best pop vocal album and best R&B performance for "Yukon."
Lauryn Hill, Reba McEntire and Post Malone

The Recording Academy has tapped multiple artists to pay tribute to the musicians who died last year during the In Memoriam segment of the ceremony. Reba McEntire will share the stage with Brandy Clark and Lukas Nelson to honor multiple musical icons. Lauryn Hill will also perform a tribute to D'Angelo and Robert Flack. To celebrate Ozzy Osbourne's legacy, Post Malone, Andrew Watt, Chad Smith, Duff McKagan and Slash will perform together.
Sabrina Carpenter

After gracing the stage last year to perform a medley of her hits “Espresso” and “Please Please Please,” Carpenter is back to sing songs from latest album, “Man’s Best Friend.” The Recording Academy announced Carpenter as the first performer this year.
Carpenter is already a two-time Grammy winner after receiving golden gramophones in 2025 for best pop vocal album for “Short n’ Sweet” and best pop solo performance for “Espresso.”

When the 2026 Grammy nominations were revealed in November, Carpenter received six nods in categories like album of the year, song of the year and record of the year. Fans will have to tune in on Feb. 1 to find out if she adds more Grammy wins to her resume.
Clipse and Pharrell Williams
Clipse, a hip-hop duo nominated for five Grammys this year, will team up with Pharrell Williams for an epic performance.

Williams, a 13-time Grammy winner, received four nods. Clipse — who consists of Gene “Malice” and Terrence “Pusha T” Thornton — and Williams collaborated on “Let God Sort Em Out,” which is nominated for album of the year and best rap album.
They also worked together on the track “The Birds Don’t Sing,” up for best rap song, and “Chains & Whips,” a best rap performance nominee.
Best New Artists
All of the best new artist nominees are performing at the 2026 Grammys. The list includes:
After getting her start on TikTok, Addison Rae released her self-titled debut album, “Addison,” in June 2025. The album includes hits like “Fame is a Gun” and “Diet Pepsi.”

Alex Warren began his career by sharing his music on YouTube and TikTok before his song “Ordinary” became a huge success last year. The song, which landed on top of the Billboard Hot 100 chart, was the lead single from his debut studio album, “You’ll Be Alright Kid.”

Girl group Katseye, with members Daniela Avanzini, Lara Raj, Manon Bannerman, Megan Skiendiel, Sophia Laforteza and Yoonchae Jeong, was formed on the 2023 reality competition show “Dream Academy.” The following year, the group released singles, “Debut” and “Touch,” and have skyrocketed since then. Their second EP, “Beautiful Chaos,” arrived in June 2025 and featured hits like “Gnarly” and “Gabriela.”

Leon Thomas, whose fans have followed his journey since his “Victorious” days, is already a Grammy winner thanks to SZA’s hit “Snooze,” which he co-wrote and won best R&B song at the 2024 Grammys. Now, he has received six Grammy nominations for his album “MUTT.” In addition to best new artist, he is also vying for best R&B album, best traditional R&B performance, best R&B song, best R&B performance and album of the year.

Oliva Dean has fans crooning along to her music all year long. The British breakout star achieved global success last year thanks to her earworms “Man I Need” and “So Easy (To Fall in Love).” Both songs are from her second album, “The Art of Loving,” which has steadily been climbing its way up the Billboard 200 chart. In November, Dean was the musical guest on “Saturday Night Live.”

Alternative artist Sombr, who is also a record producer, has an MTV Video Music Award and is looking to add a Grammy to his collection of trophies. Last year, he released his debut studio album, “I Barely Know Her,” with the hits “Back to Friends” and “Undressed.” The music video for “Back to Friends” earned him a VMA for best alternative video.

Bilingual pop band The Marías — lead singer María Zardoya, drummer Josh Conway, guitarist Jesse Perlman and keyboardist Edward James — has been making music for around a decade. But, their breakout moment came in 2022 when they were featured on Bad Bunny’s song “Otro Atardecer.” Then, their song “No One Noticed” from their second album, “Submarine,” went viral. In 2025, the group also worked with Selena Gomez and Benny Blanco on the song “Ojos Tristes” from the couple’s joint album.

British artist Lola Young, who recently took a break from performing to focus on her health, is the final best new artist nominee. Her song “Messy” from the 2024 album “This Wasn’t Meant for You Anyway” went viral on TikTok after years of making music. She performed the hit on “The Tonight Show Starring Jimmy Fallon” in January 2025. She was also featured on fellow 2026 Grammy nominee Tyler, the Creator’s song “Like Him” from his album “Chromakopia.”

title: Rob Gronkowski Relaxes His ‘Tight End’ for Prostate Cancer Screening Super Bowl Ad
summary: Super Bowl champions Bruce Arians and Rob Gronkowski star in a Super Bowl ad to encourage men to get screened for prostate cancer with an easy blood test.
story: Super Bowl champions Bruce Arians and Rob Gronkowski star in a cheeky Super Bowl ad from Novartis designed to encourage men to get screened for prostate cancer with an easy blood test.
Arians and Gronkowski appeared on TODAY Jan. 29 as paid spokespeople for the Novartis campaign to exclusively reveal the ad ahead of the Feb. 8 game.

The ad stars current NFL tight ends George Kittle (San Francisco 49ers) and Colby Parkinson (Los Angeles Rams) joining well-known retired tight ends Gronkowski (Tampa Bay Buccaneers), Greg Olsen (Carolina Panthers), Tony Gonzalez (Atlanta Falcons), Delanie Walker (Tennessee Titans) and Vernon Davis (San Francisco 49ers). The group of players relaxes with yoga, in a pool, painting and more while Enya’s “Only Time” plays underneath.
“You know what these tight ends are so relaxed about?” Arians says as he appears onscreen. “Prostate cancer screenings. They’ve learned there’s a simple, finger-free blood test.”

Arians, 73, who led the Tampa Bay Buccaneers to victory in Super Bowl 55 with Gronkowski at tight end, reminds viewers that roughly one in eight men will be diagnosed with prostate cancer in their lifetime, noting he is among that number. He had surgery for prostate cancer in 2007.
“I’m here today because I caught it early,” he continues.
Gronkowski, who won three other Super Bowls as a member of the New England Patriots, meanwhile, can be seen calmly brushing a horse.
“Gronk, what’d I teach you?” Arians asks.
“Relax your tight end,” Gronkowski replies before we see a shot of several posteriors loosening up as the clip comes to a (rear) end. “Prostate screenings starts with a simple blood test.”

Several medical groups recommend most men talk to their doctors about prostate screening beginning at age 45, although it could be 40 for those in high risk groups. Despite that, roughly 40.3 million men who are age-eligible have never had a prostate-specific antigen (PSA) blood test, according to a news release from Novartis.
Arians told TODAY he's "doing fantastic" and gets his PSA checked annually. Still, he believes that more men need to get checked.

"I think the stereotype of the finger test scares too many guys off," he said. "But when you look at early detection and saving lives, it's the best way to do it because this is a disease that's killing too many men that shouldn't be killing them."
Craig Melvin noted that Al Roker is a prostate cancer survivor as are some of his own relatives, before Gronkowski reiterated the importance of screening.
"Prostate cancer is one of the most common cancers among men in the U.S., and men fear to get screened because of the finger tests," he said.

"And we're here to raise awareness that it's just as simple as a blood test with Novartis here to get screened and see if you have prostate cancer or if it's developing," he added. "And you want to catch it early, as well, because if you catch it early, that's when it's most treatable."
For more screening information, the campaign encourages viewers to visit RelaxItsABloodTest.com.

title: Read With Jenna's February 2026 Pick Is a Love Story You'll Read in 1 Sitting
summary: Read With Jenna's February 2026 pick is "One & Only" by Maurene Goo.
story: Read With Jenna's February 2026 pick arrives just in time for Valentine's Day.

"'One & Only' by Maurene Goo is the perfect book for February. Not only is February the month of love, and this is a romance, but it's also one of the shortest months of the year — and this book flies," Jenna Bush Hager said. "You'll open it and you'll read it in one sitting."

In "One & Only," out Feb. 3, Cassia Park is a matchmaker who works for her family’s thriving Los Angeles business. The Park women have a secret: They can find a person’s fated based on their past lives.

"She believes in soulmates because it's her family business. She comes from a long line of women who can predict people's fated soulmate. Cassia knows who her soulmate is, and yet he can't be found," Jenna said.
"This is a book about fate versus free will, love and commitment, mother and daughters. It has a great love triangle. It's about writing our own story," she added
With "One & Only," Goo, a YA novelist, makes her adult debut. Speaking to TODAY, Goo said she's been seeking out books that make her happy. So, she decided to write one.
"All I knew was I really want to write something with romance at its core. I want it to be joyful and fun," she said.
Goo started to think about what else she wanted in a book: A protagonist in her 40s, a family element. But what was the story?
"I was sitting there thinking, I was like, 'Just stop thinking so hard. Just let the idea come to you.' And it came to me!" she says.
"One & Only" by Maurene Goo
Enter: the "sprinkle of magic" that sets the story apart.
"I was thinking of a magic specific to Korean culture — matchmaking. They have have all these practices going back generations and generations," Goo said.

In the novel, the Park women can actually look into the past and see people’s soulmates. So while the book's protagonist knows who her own fated is, the man — Daniel Nam — can't be found. While she awaits her destiny, she ends up meeting an unexpected match in a younger man. Then she meets her new boyfriend's boss: Daniel. (Gasp!)
While reading, Goo said she hopes people really interrogate what they believe about soulmates.
"What if I had a soulmate and I knew it. What would I do about that? Would I believe it?" she said. "What actually is important in a love story?"

title: 'Bridgerton' Season 4 Is a 'Cinderella' Re-Telling. The Stars Explain its Spin on the Fairy Tale
summary: Bridgerton stars and showrunners break down how Bridgerton Season 4, about Sophie and BEnedict, is a Cinderella re-telling.
story: A beautiful stranger, a private romantic rendezvous, a midnight getaway and a missing accessory.
Sound familiar?

"Bridgerton's" highly anticipated fourth season — which so far closely follows the plot of Julia Quinn's third "Bridgerton" book, "An Offer from a Gentleman" — has all the fixtures of your classic "Cinderella" story.
Led by Luke Thompson, who has played second eldest son Benedict Bridgerton since Season 1, Season 4 follows Benedict's love story with show newcomer Yerin Ha as a mysterious woman named Sophie Baek.
For the first time in the show, the romantic leads are of markedly different classes: Benedict is the brother of a viscount while Sophie is just a maid (or is she?)

After their chance meeting at a masquerade ball, Benedict seemingly throws out his earlier convictions of staying single and sets his sights, instead, on finding the beautiful stranger who left behind a silver glove after dashing away at midnight.
Here are the references to the classic fairy tale "Cinderella" found in "Bridgerton" Season 4 so far — and how they turn some on completely on its head.
A Masquerade Ball

Like the classic Cinderella story, "Bridgerton" Season 4's love interests meet at a ball, specifically Benedict's mom Violet's annual masquerade ball. The event formally kicks off the Ton's social season.

Though he’s reluctant to attend at first, Benedict soon meets someone intriguing: A masked woman wearing silver. Viewers soon learn she's the illegitimate child of the late Earl of Penwood, raised as a maid by her stepmother, Lady Araminta Gun (Katie Leung).
After a flirtatious private moment outside, the lady in silver dashes away, leaving behind not a shoe, but a glove, setting Benedict on a quest to find her.
His journey eventually overlaps with Sophie in the countryside — but like the classic tale, he doesn't recognize her.
"It's the romance genre. It is a bit of a fantasy. Is it a little ridiculous that he doesn't recognize her? Sure, absolutely," "Bridgerton" showrunner Jess Brownell tells TODAY.com.
"However, I do think that it's really important not to underestimate the power of class in that time period," she adds.
Even someone who thinks of themselves as "progressive" — like Benedict — wouldn't conceive of meeting "a maid at a ball," Brownell says.

"You wouldn't meet a maid who you would fall in love with because they were just marginalized in such an incredible way," she says. "So I think like that inability of his to recognize her is also part of the story, and part of what he needs to reckon with in himself, absolutely."
A Handsome ‘Prince’
Of course, by prince we mean rake.
Like his brothers and brother-in-law before him, Benedict is the latest Bridgerton on the Ton to be dubbed a rake, defined as a man who indulges in vices and generally avoids romantic commitment.

Season 3 saw Benedict continue his quest to live "outside" of the regimented Regency-era society of his family. This finally culminated in his relationship with Tilley Arnold (Hannah New), a  witty, financially independent noblewoman. Through Tilley, he meets Paul, and the trio has a threesome, confirming longtime fan speculation that Benedict is queer.
Season 3 ended with Benedict rejecting Tilley's request for a more traditional relationship, telling her that after she "opened his world," he doesn't want to close it again.
What is it about the lady in silver?
Brownell says even before the ball, Benedict "wishes" he wanted marriage — but simply doesn't.
"I don’t think that he’s ever met anyone who challenges him or opens his worldview in a way that makes it feel worth giving up his ability to do whatever he wants at any moment in time," she says.
That's still the case when Season 4 picks up. But with just a few words, Luke Thompson tells TODAY.com, the lady in silver changes that.
"One of the best things that Sophie as the lady in silver says to Benedict is ... what's the line?" Thompson asks his co-star.
"'Will you not wade out deeper with me,'" Yerin Ha finishes.
"He's not very good at delving deep," Thompson adds. "And I think Sophie as the lady in silver pushes him, like forces him to do that. She holds him to account."
A 'Wicked Stepmother'
Like the classic Cinderella tale, Sophie has to avoid the watchful gaze of her stepmother and stepsisters at the ball.
After Lady Araminta Gun's husband, the Earl of Penwood, died, she raised Sophie, his illegitimate daughter introduced simply as his "ward," to be a maid.
"If they looked on the surface, they would probably see her as the evil wicked stepmother, whatever you want to call that," Katie Leung tells TODAY.com.
But she argues the reality is more complicated.
In a flashback, viewers see Lady Araminta after she marries the earl and arrives at her new home with her two daughters in tow, full of enthusiasm.

Leung says while they don't know much about Araminta's first marriage, which resulted in daughters Rosamund and Posy, she and Brownell spoke about her second, establishing that the marriage was "definitely for love."
So when Araminta is introduced to Sophie, a child she didn't know about, her smile instantly drops.
"She's excited about the prospect of them living together," she says. "It's such a small moment, but it's so integral to to what could have been."
The goal was to show Araminta's "vulnerability and softness" in contrast to the stakes of keeping the secret of Sophie's parentage.

"Being a woman in that era, trying to maintain this kind of reputation, and being a single mom with two kids that you're trying to marry off ... and not having a son, having shame within that," Leung says.

"All the while you have this deep, dark secret that if anybody found out, then your whole reputation would be tarnished, and you and your daughters wouldn't have a future — that's how cutthroat it would have been, and could be, for Araminta."

title: ‘Shrinking’ Stars Reflect on Returning to Altadena for Season 3 After LA Fires
summary: “Shrinking” is back for its third season. Read what the stars told TODAY.com about filming on location following the devastating Los Angeles fires.
story: “Shrinking” is back for its third season, and the return of the Apple TV show has some feeling nostalgic.

The series follows grieving therapist Jimmy (Jason Segel) after the death of his wife. Meanwhile, his teenage daughter Alice (Lukita Maxwell) navigates the loss of her mother, too. As the seasons continue, the poignant show also tackles the progression of Dr. Paul's (Harrison Ford) Parkinson’s disease and how all the characters come together to take care of each other. (Ahead of its Jan. 27 Season 3 premiere, the Apple TV show was already renewed for a fourth season.)
Season 3 may be especially noteworthy and emotional for residents of Altadena and Pasadena, who were affected by the Eaton Fire that erupted on Jan. 7, 2025.

Nineteen people died, thousands were displaced and 9,418 homes and other buildings were destroyed when the Eaton Canyon caught on fire during 90 miles per hour winds, according to The Associated Press. At the same time, the Palisades Fire on the opposite side of town was also ablaze and destructive.
“Shrinking” is filmed in Los Angeles and highlights special landmarks in the Pasadena and Altadena communities.

Locations like Gaby’s (Jessica Williams) home in Altadena, where they shoot exterior shots, was burned down. Eaton Canyon, where the fire ignited, is also used as a hiking location for the show. Jimmy and Alice's home, which is right next to Liz (Christa Miller) and Derek's (Ted McGinley)'s house, is located on the cusp of Pasadena and Altadena.

Altadena staple Charles S. Farnsworth Park, where the crew is seen playing pickleball and roaming the park, was also deeply impacted when the William D. Davies Memorial Building was destroyed. A year after the fires, the park has yet to be reopened or rebuilt.
TODAY.com spoke with the stars of the Emmy-nominated show about their experience returning to the set of “Shrinking” following the devastating fire.
The below has been edited and condensed for clarity.
Jessica Williams

I always think about what Mr. Rogers said, where he’s like, “Look for the helpers in the time of disaster.” And to me, with both fires, especially being from LA, it was just devastating. To be able to go back and hire locally and be in local places, donate and give time, that was a big, big part of my personal journey when shooting the show.

I think we were supposed to start shooting the week of the fires. So we had to push and then we had to do all the interior (shots). I know the homeowners of my character’s house. It burned down. So Gaby’s house burned down — so we just shot (it on a sound stage) — which is so, so sad. There was a bit of fear of wanting to get it right and figuring out how to play the show with the fires and just choosing to kind of move forward on the show. I was really nervous about it.

There were so many starts and stops because we couldn’t shoot outside in the area, especially at Jimmy and Liz’s house with the air quality, which is in Pasadena. As someone who lives in Eagle Rock, the best part is shooting in our community and area, and it was just really sad. All the actors felt it. A few of the actors weren’t in their homes and some of the crew lost their homes. But (we were like), we got to make money. At that point, there’s only like, five projects going in LA. There’s not a lot that shoots in LA. We’re starting to get that back, but it was just sad and it was kind of just the backdrop of the whole show.
Lukita Maxwell

I live not too far and it was a very difficult time. We didn’t go back on location for a while. We were just waiting to make sure that the location was clear and safe for everybody to go to and that everybody there was safe. I remember the first drive back onto the set and — as it is with, I think, all of these areas in LA that were affected and touched by this crazy fire — the energy of the Earth changed. So there was a lot of energy on that drive. I remember feeling very grateful that I was and my loved ones were safe. It was a hard time.
Michael Urie

I was so, so sad about Altadena when we started shooting last season and so much of it was gone. So many people were displaced. And our sweet locations, we have a lot of locations in Altadena, and luckily, almost all of them were OK. Going back there was very moving for all of us because we couldn’t for a while. We had to postpone a lot of those scenes for the end of the shoot, but being back there was quite moving.

(Before “Shrinking”), I didn’t know about this neighborhood. I had slept on Altadena. It was like this magical little jewel in the Los Angeles neighborhood spectrum that had slipped past me. It is really lovely there, and I’m so glad that we got to bring some life and some money back to the community. It’s become just a beautiful location for our show. I think we’ve gotten to give a face to a name to a lot of people that might not know that community.
Ted McGinley
I lived in the Palisades, so we’re not in our house either. We moved out to Altadena, where we film, and we couldn’t go there because the houses were being remediated. Thank God, they made it.

We’re very proud of how we represent Pasadena, Altadena. It’s a great community. They’ve opened their doors to us and we try to respect it. And you see it in the show, we show the best parts of it and it’s a great background. We had to wait to get into our houses, so we did the interiors. And then when we shot the exteriors, we did all of the (scenes). We did like six, seven shows in one week, in the back, in the outside. But we couldn’t get in for months, we were behind... It was really tricky, but it was a fun challenge. We love the people of Pasadena, Altadena.
Luke Tennie

(Returning to Altadena and Pasadena) felt great — and let me explain why. In my light research, I’ve learned how beneficial it can be to work in places that have experienced natural disasters. I grew up in South Florida, so our disasters look different, a lot of hurricanes and rain and tropical storms. There’d be a lot of damage there. But the most important thing about where we live is what takes place here. The industry that makes a lot of the money here is film and TV. So the more that we can shoot in these locations, the more we can nurse them back to health. “Shrinking” is a Pasadena show. It’s an Altadena show. It takes place there. The more we can actually operate there and create work there, and bring revenue and jobs there, the better the place will be. So I hope that we can do, I don’t know, maybe about 87 or 89 more seasons of “Shrinking,” to be specific. I think that’ll all just help the city out in terms of growing and repairing the damage that was done.

title: ‘The Pitt’s’ Gerran Howell Was ‘Peer Pressured’ into Pranking George Clooney — and Is ‘Terrified’ for Him to Find Out
summary: “The Pitt” star Gerran Howell is owning up to a prank he pulled on George Clooney — and is sharing why he is “terrified” for the Oscar winner to learn he was the culprit.
story: “The Pitt” star Gerran Howell is owning up to a prank he pulled on George Clooney — and is sharing why he is “terrified” for the Oscar winner to learn he was the culprit.

Howell, who portrays Dr. Dennis Whitaker on the hit HBO medical drama, appeared on the 3rd Hour of TODAY Jan. 28 to discuss the new season. During his chat with Al Roker, Emilie Ikeda and Craig Melvin, Howell also spoke about how he and “The Pitt” co-star Noah Wyle both previously worked with Clooney.

While Wyle and Clooney shared the screen during their “ER” days, Howell teamed up with the “Jay Kelly” star more recently when they starred together in the miniseries “Catch-22,” which was released in 2019.
When Al asked the Welsh actor about Clooney directing a couple “Catch-22” episodes, the TODAY co-host mentioned that he heard Howell participated in a prank on set.
“Oh, dear. Well, I was peer pressured into it may I first say,” Howell replied. “It was ‘Catch-22,’ so we were working on an Air Force base in Italy, and we had bikes to get around everywhere.”

“We went to a supermarket, and there were My Little Pony bike accessories. And someone said, ‘Wouldn’t that be funny if you put some glittery streamers on George’s bike one lunchtime and a lovely pink bell?’” he recalled. “So I did that, and then I remember riding onto set, and he had clearly ripped them off.”
Howell continued, “Nothing was said about it, so I am terrified that maybe my life is I’m just waiting for him (to find out).
The 34-year-old said he had never publicly shared the story, so Clooney didn’t know he was behind the prank … until now.
“Your first and last time working with George Clooney," Craig joked.
Howell responded, “There we go. Exactly,” as he laughed.

In “Catch-22,” which was based on the 1961 novel of the same name by Joseph Heller, Howell portrayed Kid Sampson in five episodes. Clooney only appeared in three episodes as Scheisskopf, however, he also directed two episodes and served as an executive producer on the series.

title: Kim Kardashian Explains Why Pics of Meghan and Harry Were Deleted From Kris Jenner's Birthday Party
summary: Kim Kardashian has shared the stories behind "Photogate" involving Prince Harry and Meghan, Duchess of Sussex, at her mom Kris Jenner's 70th birthday bash.
story: Kim Kardashian has shared the stories behind "Photogate" involving Prince Harry and Meghan, Duchess of Sussex, at her mom Kris Jenner's 70th birthday bash.

The fashion mogul and reality star spoke to sister Khloe Kardashian on her "Khloé In Wonder Land" podcast released on Jan. 28 about why Kim Kardashian and Jenner deleted photos of the royal couple from the party that they had shared on Instagram in November. The sisters dubbed it "Photogate."
"It was really innocent, which is so crazy," Kim Kardashian said.

She said Jenner and the former Meghan Markle have been friends for years and have "a really sweet relationship," which led to Meghan and Harry being on the guest list with the likes of Mariah Carey and Oprah Winfrey for Jenner's star-studded birthday party.
Kim Kardashian said she asked the royal couple if it was OK to post photos of them from the night.
"We’re all communicating about like what we’re going to post," Kardashian said. "We’re never ones to post without permission. That's not who we are."

She said they initially received permission, but then requested they be taken down. The photos were posted around the time of Remembrance Day, a British memorial day that honors military veterans who have died in the line of duty. Prince Harry is a British military veteran himself.

"We were told that it was totally cool to post, and then after it was posted, I think they realized it was Remembrance Day, and they didn’t want to be seen at a party even though it’s already up, you know, and then taken down," Kardashian said. "And then I think they realized like, 'Ugh, this was so silly.'"
The royal couple had also attended the Baby2Baby gala event earlier that night to support the charity event in which their friend, tennis legend Serena Williams, was honored with an award.
"It's like a charity event, so like that was fine, but maybe not partying and dancing on the dance floor (at Jenner's party)," Kardashian said. "We took them down to respect Remembrance Day."
Jenner posted a carousel of photos on Instagram featuring many of the celebrity guests, but Harry and Meghan were absent.
Kardashian seemed to see the scrutiny over the deleted photos as an overblown issue.
"You've just got to laugh at the situation sometimes and just like lighten it up," she said. "If everyone’s taking it the wrong way like lean in."
She suggested they should've made it into a bit for her undergarment company.

"I was like we should do a full Skims campaign, take the photo, post it and then delete it 30 minutes later and say, 'Oh, sorry. I didn’t have permission to post those photos for the campaign,'" she said. "That would have hit so funny if we just made it light and made it funny. I think it would've been received differently.
"But I hated how that was received for everyone," she continued. "It was just made into something that was so crazy and ridiculous that didn't have to be."
TODAY.com has reached out to the Sussexes for comment and has not heard back by the time of publication.

title: Serena Williams Has Cheeky Reaction When Asked If She's Returning to Tennis
summary: Is Serena Williams returning to tennis? Here's how she responded when asked about her retirement plans during a TODAY interview.
story: Is the greatest women's tennis player of all time considering a return to the sport?
Serena Williams, who stepped away from the sport in 2022, wasn't about to give that away on TODAY on Jan. 28, but she didn't say no, either.
"I mean really, are you asking this on the TODAY show?" she told Savannah Guthrie. "Oh my God."
"They would kill me if I didn't," Savannah replied.

Williams, 44, raised some eyebrows in December when she filed the necessary paperwork to return to the sport's registered drug-testing pool. With speculation building at that time, she tweeted, "Omg yall I’m NOT coming back. This wildfire is crazy."
On TODAY, she was not that definitive.
"Oh my goodness," she said.
"Is that a no?" Savannah asked.
"Is that a no?" Williams responded. "Now people on the set are laughing, this is distracting. You're distracting us over there."
"OK, but you didn't say yes or no," Savannah said.
"I'm just having fun and enjoying my life right now," Williams replied.
"That's not a yes or a no," Savannah clarified.
"That's not a yes or a no," Williams said. "I don't know, I'm just going to see what happens."
Savannah stayed on the case.
"That's a maybe to me," she said.
"That's not a maybe," Williams replied. "Listen, I have two kids. I am a full-time stay-at-home (mom). When I filled out a form the other day, occupation: housewife."
Savannah followed by noting that Williams re-entered the drug protocol, a precursor for any type of comeback by the 23-time Grand Slam winner.

There also was conjecture that she would return because her sister, fellow tennis luminary Venus Williams, was back on the court at 45, where she recently became the oldest woman to compete in the singles draw at the Australian Open.
"Did I re-enter?" Williams coyly said about the drug protocol. "I didn't know if I was out. Listen, I can't discuss this."
"I'm just saying that if you wanted to put it to bed, this would be a good moment to put the retirement rumors to bed," Savannah said.
"I want to go to bed," Williams said while laughing. "It's early. You're funny. That was fun."
Craig Melvin was unconvinced, as he said from off camera that he "heard a maybe."
"You didn't hear a maybe," Williams said.

Williams played her final match in September 2022 after participating in her final Grand Slam event at that year's U.S. Open. She shared in an essay for Vogue at that time that she was choosing motherhood over continuing her career.
She is a mom to daughters Olympia, 8, and Adira, 2, with husband Alexis Ohanian.

Williams also spoke to Savannah about Venus' wedding over the holidays and her upcoming Super Bowl commercial for Ro, a health care company connected to a GLP-1 medication that she says has transformed her health. Williams' husband, Alexis Ohanian, is an investor in Ro and serves on the board.

title: A Kentucky Derby-Inspired Movie Filmed at Churchill Downs Is Coming to Hallmark
summary: Hallmark is teaming up with Churchill Downs to make a Kentucky Derby-inspired movie filmed at the historic racetrack. Everything you need to know.
story: Hallmark has revealed it is teaming up with Churchill Downs to make a Kentucky Derby-inspired movie filmed at the historic racetrack.

The network shared exclusively with TODAY that its new original film, “Kentucky Roses,” will honor the “pageantry, tradition, and enduring spirit of the Kentucky Derby.” The movie will premiere on Hallmark Saturday, May 2, the same day of the 2026 Kentucky Derby. The sporting event is famously held the first Saturday in May every year at Churchill Downs.
“Kentucky Roses,” which will also stream the next day on Hallmark+, will star Andrew Walker and Odette Annable, as well as Ally Ledford, Peyton Meyer, Brynn Thayer and Gregg Henry.

The film’s storyline will take place across two timelines. Sadie Moore, played by Annable, will be at the center of the story in present day. She works at a Churchill Downs greenhouse and dreams of becoming a florist and following in her great-grandmother’s (Ledford) footsteps by supplying the Derby with a display of roses. Sadie then meets Ash Taylor, Walker’s character, who is the son of the Churchill Downs CEO (Henry). Ash has to repair one of the racetrack’s iconic Twin Spires after it is damaged in a storm.
Sadie and Ash develop a connection, but it could be cut short after they discover “a bittersweet love story from the past that threatens to repeat itself.”

Speaking about the collaboration between Hallmark and Churchill Downs, Jessica Callahan, vice president at Hallmark Media, said in part, “Together, we’re thrilled to bring audiences an inspiring story of community, courage and time-honored traditions.”

Along with the romantic flick, Hallmark and Churchill Downs are also partnering to release a collection of jewelry inspired by “Kentucky Roses.” The jewelry will be sold in Hallmark Gold Crown stores.

title: The Real-Life Love Story That Inspired Alex Warren's 'Ordinary'
summary: What is the meaning of Alex Warren's song "Ordinary"? Here's how his wife inspired the hit song.
story: Alex Warren turned everyday love into the hit song "Ordinary."
Released in February 2025, the song was practically everywhere in 2025, topping Billboard's Hot 100 and being named as Billboard's 2025 song of the summer. This is Warren's first No. 1 hit song.
Warren debuted his first album, "You’ll Be Alright, Kid," on July 18 with feature tracks from Jelly Roll and BLACKPINK singer ROSÉ.
Going into the new year, Warren reached a new milestone in 2026: his first Grammy nomination for best new artist. He will also perform at the award show on Feb. 1.

The 24-year-old did it all with the woman who inspired the song by his side. Warren tied the knot with YouTuber Kouvr Annon in 2024. At that point, couple had been together for four years. Warren decided to pursue a career in music after they both left Hype House, a content creator house.
Annon appears in the music video for the song, which shows him following her through a wooded landscape.
Below, find more about the meaning of "Ordinary," and be prepared with tissues.
The Meaning Behind the Lyrics of 'Ordinary'
"Ordinary" is a pop ballad about being deeply in love.

Warren makes his heavy emotions clear early on in the song as he sings, "Oh, my, my love/ I take one look at you/ You’re takin’ me out of the ordinary/ I want you layin’ me down ‘til we’re dead and buried."
The song uses spiritual imagery and metaphors to describe the experience. In an interview with HITS, Warren said as he is a Christian and often uses biblical references in his music.
The chorus goes, "The angels up in the clouds are jealous knowin’ we found/Hopeless hallelujah/On this side of Heaven’s gate...At your altar, I will pray."

As the song continues, Warren sings about how out of the ordinary this life-changing love is: "World was in black and white until I saw your light/I thought you had to die to find/Somethin’ so out of the ordinary."
Warren shared on Instagram that he wrote the song about his wife.
"I wrote ordinary about my wife, my best friend," he wrote. "It’s crazy to think that we’ve been together for almost 7 years."
“This song is now number 1 everywhere and I can’t help but smile to know it’s about you,” Warren wrote. “It also reminds me that we’ve been together this long and I’ve never seen you run full speed.”
Warren told the music publication HITS more about the origin story of the song, which he co-wrote with Adam Yaron, Cal Shapiro and Mags Duval.

“We wrote that record about my relationship with my wife, Kouvr. We’re both 24, we met when we were 18, she slept in a car with me. I think it’s rare today to find true love, with phones and social media and all these different things. And so we wrote ‘Ordinary’ about how love is falling out, but we found something crazy,” he said.
In a TikTok video, Warren gently roasted Annon for not knowing the lyrics to the song. "I thought the lyrics said, 'And I'll my life I'll do ya,'" she said in the video.
“I thought you were talking about getting freaky, and I was, like, ‘Damn, my mom listens to this,’” she continued.
The real lyrics go, “Oh, my life, how do ya?/ Breathe and take my breath away?”
Read “Ordinary” lyrics in full below.
Read the Lyrics of 'Ordinary' in Full
They say, “The holy water’s watered down
And this town’s lost its faith
Our colors will fade eventually.”
So, if our time is runnin’ out
Day after day
We’ll make the mundane our masterpiece

You’re takin’ me out of the ordinary
I want you layin’ me down ‘til we’re dead and buried
On the edge of your knife, stayin’ drunk on your vine
The angels up in the clouds are jealous knowin’ we found
Somethin’ so out of the ordinary
You got me kissin’ thе ground of your sanctuary
Shatter me with your touch, oh, Lord, return mе to dust
The angels up in the clouds are jealous knowin’ we found
Hopeless hallelujah
On this side of Heaven’s gate
Oh, my life, how do ya
Breathe and take my breath away?

You’re the sculptor, I’m the clay

You got me kissing the ground (Ground) of your sanctuary (Sanctuary)

title: Chrissy Teigen Reveals the 'Quiet' Warning Signs She Was Drinking Too Much Alcohol
summary: Chrissy Teigen says she has quit alcohol again after becoming concerned she was drinking too much. She reveals the "quiet" signs that concerned her.
story: Chrissy Teigen says she has quit alcohol again after becoming concerned she was drinking too much.
“We aren’t talking the kind of drinking where you slur your words and miss a step on the stairs. It was just quiet and consistent,” Teigen, 40, revealed in a post on Instagram on Tuesday, Jan. 27.
The model and cookbook author has now been sober for more than 50 days, she wrote.
She first took a break from drinking in 2020, then celebrated a year without alcohol in 2022.
But in the spring of 2025, Teigen said she let alcohol back into her life.
“I promised myself it would be in a ‘mindful’ way. I wanted to be able to have a glass of wine on a date night with my husband. To be able to toast on birthdays,” she wrote in her post this week.
“Soon it became the kind of drinking I’m all too familiar with. Quiet moments at home were for wine.”
Teigen noticed that she began to drink earlier and earlier in the day, first starting at 6 p.m. then eventually at lunch. “When it got to 11am, I was like, oh f--k, here we go again,” she wrote.
The day drinking meant she wasn’t able to pick up her car keys for a last-minute grocery run, she added, another troubling sign. And she didn't feel good about herself.

Still, it wasn’t “messy enough yet to stop,” Teigen said. What made the difference was a conversation on her "Self-Conscious" podcast with “Summer House” star Carl Radke about his own journey with sobriety.
She was also in talks to appear on the “Star Search” reboot and vowed there'd be "absolutely no f-----g way I’d do it with alcohol in my dressing room."
Teigen said she has no desire to turn back and return to drinking alcohol.
“Turns out, without it, I can still be funny. Still be stupid. Still be wildly nervous. Anxious," she wrote. "And I can get through it all without it.”

title: Ben Stiller and Benson Boone Are an Unlikely Duo in Instacart’s Super Bowl Commercial
summary: Ben Stiller and Benson Boone play brothers in an '80s-style Europop band in a Super Bowl ad for Instacart, directed by Spike Jonze.
story: Ben Stiller goes bananas in every comedic role he embodies, but this time, it’s little more literal.

The “Zoolander” actor and “Severance” director teamed up with the “Mystical Magical” singer with more flips than a home-design show, Benson Boone, for Instacart’s Super Bowl commercial. The vintage-style spot, entitled “Bananas,” was directed by Spike Jonze, who directed the movies “Her,” “Being John Malkovich” and a plethora of iconic music videos.

“What was fun about it, to me, was committing to a very specific world and, comedically, it’s fun to jump into something like that,” Stiller tells TODAY.com, adding that the combination of working with Boone and Jonze — whom he calls “one of our great American directors” — sounded really exciting to him.

“I’ve known Spike for a long time, but we never worked together, and I knew that if Spike was doing it, he was going to dial it in a super specific way, and just committing to the ridiculousness of it and the physicality of it was really fun,” he says. “I had a blast, dude.”

The pair’s star power shines throughout the 30-second spot that sees Stiller and Boone as Gary and John, heavily accented brothers and bandmates with “Dodgeball”-worthy mustaches. As with many of Stiller’s characters who become annoyed to their own detriment, his clear jealousy over Boone’s high falsetto and flapjack-like nature come to a cartoonish head.

The duo’s ’80s Europop band sings and dances to introduce Instacart’s latest in-app product innovation: Preference Picker. Starting with bananas, the new tool lets customers select their preferred ripeness — from “not ripe” to “almost ripe” to “ripe” — so shoppers can make better real-time decisions on their behalf. Hopefully, it means your “Heated Rivalry”-style smoothie will be safe from green bananas.

“It’s like, when you go into a supermarket, you can go and squeeze the fruit, make sure it’s the one you like, so I thought that this was a cool idea,” Stiller says. “And then for me, the jingle was so catchy and so, so ridiculous … It was a simple idea.”

Stiller’s Europop big brother character could fit into any one of his movies and is further boosted by Jonze’s meticulous attention to detail. The commercial was all shot on vintage tube cameras  — the same kind of camera used to shoot countless variety shows like the one it emulates — to “heighten the retro disco energy” of the comedic performance.

The 60-foot, glittering stage illuminated with orange and green LED lighting was built specifically for this ad, which of course, was littered with flips — more from Stiller than Boone, it should be noted.

“First of all, let me say, if anybody’s annoyed with his flipping, it’s flip envy,” Stiller says of the discourse around Boone’s onstage persona. “Nobody could do what he does. Come on. It’s crazy. I’ve seen it up close. It’s insane.”
Stiller also says he couldn’t deny Boone’s talent when they recorded the jingle together and he “heard him go off and do his crazy run.”
“He’s just so good, and it comes so naturally to him, and he’s so young, and it just kind of flows out,” Stiller adds about Boone.
Boone said in a press release that working on a Super Bowl commercial “blew (his) mind,” and that “adding Ben Stiller to the equation” was “diabolical.”

Aside from a couple of already released teasers (“Goals” and “Harmonizing”), Instacart is also releasing Spike Jonze’s director’s cut, a long-form version of the ad, that expands the musical rivalry into a full two-plus-minute narrative arc.
The 30-second spot will air during the Super Bowl on Sunday, Feb. 8, in the first quarter in the U.S. and the second quarter in Canada.

title: Susan Powter Makes Final Uber Eats Delivery with Al Roker on TODAY: ‘Everything Is Next’
summary: Susan Powter has closed a difficult chapter of her life with one last Uber Eats delivery — and she took along Al Roker to witness the poignant moment.
story: Susan Powter has closed a difficult chapter of her life with one last Uber Eats delivery — and she took along Al Roker to witness the poignant moment.

The ’90s fitness guru, known for her informercials urging Americans to “stop the insanity!” with her signature blond buzzcut, has been delivering food for Uber Eats for the past nine years while living paycheck to paycheck at a small apartment in Las Vegas.

A documentary released in November about Powter’s descent from fitness fame called “Stop the Insanity: Finding Susan Powter” has generated an extraordinary outpouring of support and opened a new world of possibilities for the 68-year-old mother of three. The film is available to watch on demand.
It has prompted her to leave her days of delivering for Uber Eats behind. She took Al along on Jan. 23 when she made the last of her deliveries in Las Vegas, which was shared on Jan. 28 on TODAY.
Al asked her if she ever thought she would have a chance to reinvent herself like this.
“I had lost hope,” she said. “I never thought about Susan Powter, ‘stop the insanity’ Susan Powter. I never thought about that. It was just was gone for many years. It was gone.”

Powter said on TODAY in November that a combination of bad business deals, lawsuits and a second divorce resulted in her declaring personal bankruptcy in 1995 after helming a fitness empire worth tens of millions.
She then disappeared from the spotlight for 30 years until the documentary, directed by Zeberiah Newman and executive produced by Jamie Lee Curtis, put a spotlight on her story.

“This is what I want people to really know,” she told Craig Melvin, Savannah Guthrie and Al on TODAY Jan. 28. “Possibility. A chance. Just being able to look forward to something is what really heals.”

Al’s ride with Powter also gave a glimpse into the inner workings of being a driver for a food delivery app. At one point, Powter picked up a Starbucks order from a commercial kitchen facility rather than an actual Starbucks. The facilities have been created as one-stop shops for delivery drivers for pickups.
She shared that she made $2.20 on the Starbucks delivery to an apartment. Al asked her if there was an aspect of making the deliveries that she would miss.
“No. I wouldn’t say ‘miss.’ I will never not acknowledge how grateful I am to have been able to do this,” she said.
The release of the documentary provided her with optimism when it was in short supply.

“I wrote seven books. I did nine videos. I did that work. And I thought it meant nothing,” she told Al. “When I first did the show, TODAY show, the movie wasn’t even out. And I remember saying, ‘All this love was out there, Susan.’
“And I’m seeing that in real time online. I’m hearing that. You’re sitting here. We’re doing this. That is the most healing thing for me.”
